aesops-fables.name: Aesop's Fables
aesops-fables.description:
The timeless moral fables attributed to Aesop, a legendary storyteller of ancient Greece (c. 620-564 BCE). Through talking animals, cunning foxes, proud lions, and foolish asses, these 284 short tales distill universal truths about human nature into stories children have loved for 2,500 years. V.S. Vernon Jones translation with introduction by G.K. Chesterton. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #21 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/21). 

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur Rackham's 1912 illustrations for 'Aesop's Fables' (Heinemann) — widely available in public domain, featuring pen-and-ink drawings of foxes, lions, wolves, and other characters. Milo Winter's 1919 illustrations for 'The Aesop for Children' (Rand McNally) — full-color illustrations, public domain. Walter Crane's 1887 'The Baby's Own Aesop' — woodcut-style illustrations with rhyming morals, public domain. Ernest Griset's 1869 illustrations for 'Aesop's Fables' (Cassell) — detailed pen-and-ink, public domain. Francis Barlow's 1666 'Aesop's Fables with His Life' — the earliest major English illustrated edition, public domain. Richard Heighway's 1894 illustrations — pen-and-ink, public domain. Harrison Weir's 1867 illustrations for 'Aesop's Fables' — naturalistic animal drawings, public domain.

alice-in-wonderland-chapter-book.name: Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland — A Chapter Book
alice-in-wonderland-chapter-book.description: Lewis Carroll's beloved classic broken into scenes for multi-level reading. Level 2 chapters are emergent summaries (perfect for the littlest readers). Level 1 scenes contain the COMPLETE ORIGINAL TEXT alongside age-adapted retellings. Based on Sir John Tenniel's 1865 public domain illustrations.

alice-s-tarot-my-5-years-old.name: Alice's Tarot (My 5 Years Old)
alice-s-tarot-my-5-years-old.description: This is a joint endeavour between myself and my daughter to create a tarot deck. It will mix Portuguese and English language, as we do at home :)

anon-shiryo-cat-stories.name: Anon's Robot Stories: Shiryo & Bishisheshi
anon-shiryo-cat-stories.description: A collection of children's stories where Anon — a genderless, ageless parent learning through life — watches their robot Shiryo learn to care for a kitten named Bishisheshi. Each story teaches a relational grammar: a pattern of how to be with others and with nature. Designed for recursive AI-assisted creation — each story cover acts as a generative seed containing enough context (values, characters, visual references, symbolic roots) for an AI collaborator to generate missing images and text while maintaining the soul of the story.

archetypal-astrology-tarnas.name: Archetypal Astrology — Richard Tarnas
archetypal-astrology-tarnas.description:
A grammar of planetary archetypes based on Richard Tarnas's archetypal astrology, as developed in 'Cosmos and Psyche' (2006) and his lectures at the California Institute of Integral Studies. Unlike traditional astrology, Tarnas's framework centers on the PLANETS as primary archetypal forces — not the zodiac signs. Each planet embodies a multivalent archetype drawn from Greek mythology, depth psychology, and the history of ideas. The power of the system lies in planetary ASPECTS — when two or more planetary archetypes combine (through conjunction, opposition, square, or trine), they produce emergent archetypal dynamics that can be observed in both individual psychology and collective cultural events.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Classical paintings of the planetary deities: Botticelli's 'Birth of Venus', Caravaggio's 'Jupiter, Neptune, and Pluto', Velázquez's 'Mars', Rubens's 'Saturn Devouring His Son', Raphael's 'The School of Athens' (Mercury/Hermes). Giovanni Battista Tiepolo's ceiling frescoes of planetary gods (18th century). Ancient Roman frescoes and mosaics depicting planetary deities (Pompeii, Herculaneum). Medieval and Renaissance astronomical manuscripts with planetary symbols and orbits. William Blake's mythological paintings (Uranus/creative rebellion imagery). Gustave Moreau's symbolist paintings of mythological figures (Jupiter, Prometheus). NASA planetary photographs for the outer planets (public domain).

art-of-war.name: The Art of War — Sun Tzu
art-of-war.description:
The foundational text on strategy and warfare by Sun Tzu (544-496 BCE), Chinese military general, strategist, and philosopher. Thirteen chapters covering strategic planning, tactical dispositions, energy, terrain, espionage, and the art of winning without fighting. 'Supreme excellence consists in breaking the enemy's resistance without fighting.' Lionel Giles translation (1910), the definitive English edition. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #132 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/132). Commentary and footnotes by Giles have been stripped — only Sun Tzu's original verses are included.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Traditional Chinese calligraphy and brush paintings of the Art of War text. Bamboo slip manuscripts — the earliest surviving copies of Sun Tzu were written on bamboo strips (Yinqueshan Han Slips, 2nd century BCE). Chinese landscape paintings from the Song and Ming dynasties depicting military campaigns. Illustrations from early printed editions of the Art of War (Chinese woodblock prints). Japanese ukiyo-e prints depicting military strategy (Utagawa school). European military maps and battle diagrams from 18th-19th century editions.

art-with-mati-and-dada.name: Art with Mati and Dada
art-with-mati-and-dada.description: A delightful journey through art history as Mati & Dada meet famous painters across time. Fun stories, gentle pacing, and beautiful visuals introduce kids to great artists in a way that’s engaging, educational, and safe for young viewers

bulfinch-s-classical-mythology.name: Bulfinch's Classical Mythology
bulfinch-s-classical-mythology.description: Mythological stories of the gods, heroes, and constellations from Thomas Bulfinch's 'The Age of Fable' (1855). Presents the Greek and Roman myths behind the planets and zodiac signs, connecting astrology to its ancient narrative roots.

bulfinch-s-classical-mythology-202601272.name: Bulfinch's Classical Mythology
bulfinch-s-classical-mythology-202601272.description: Mythological stories of the gods, heroes, and constellations from Thomas Bulfinch's 'The Age of Fable' (1855). Presents the Greek and Roman myths behind the planets and zodiac signs, connecting astrology to its ancient narrative roots.

civil-disobedience.name: Civil Disobedience
civil-disobedience.description:
Henry David Thoreau's 'Civil Disobedience' (1849, originally 'Resistance to Civil Government') — the foundational text of nonviolent resistance. Written after Thoreau spent a night in jail for refusing to pay a poll tax that supported slavery and the Mexican-American War. This essay directly influenced Gandhi's satyagraha, Martin Luther King Jr.'s civil rights strategy, and every nonviolent resistance movement since. 'Under a government which imprisons any unjustly, the true place for a just man is also a prison.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #71 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/71)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Daguerreotypes of Thoreau. Illustrations from early editions of Walden. Concord, Massachusetts landscapes by the Hudson River School painters.

common-sense.name: Common Sense
common-sense.description:
Thomas Paine's Common Sense (1776) -- the pamphlet that made the American Revolution inevitable. In plain language that any colonist could understand, Paine argued that hereditary monarchy was absurd, that reconciliation with Britain was impossible, and that the time for independence was NOW. It sold 500,000 copies in a population of 2.5 million. 'A long habit of not thinking a thing wrong, gives it a superficial appearance of being right.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #147 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/147)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: 18th century American Revolutionary War engravings. Paul Revere's prints. John Trumbull's Declaration of Independence paintings.

communist-manifesto.name: The Communist Manifesto
communist-manifesto.description:
The Communist Manifesto (1848) by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels — the most influential political pamphlet in history. In four chapters, it argues that all history is the history of class struggle, analyzes the revolutionary role of the bourgeoisie, and calls for the abolition of private property. Placed on the 'contested' shelf: it is both a brilliant analysis of capitalism's dynamics AND a blueprint that was used to justify immense suffering. Both truths coexist.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #61 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/61)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: 19th century political cartoons and broadsheets. Honoré Daumier's lithographs of workers. Gustave Doré's illustrations of London poverty. Soviet constructivist posters (early period, pre-Stalin).

confucian-analects.name: The Confucian Analects
confucian-analects.description: The collected sayings and teachings of Confucius (Kong Qiu, 551-479 BC), compiled by his disciples. Level 1 items contain the original Legge translation text of each individual teaching. Level 2 items are the 20 traditional books with thematic summaries.

dhammapada.name: The Dhammapada
dhammapada.description: The Dhammapada ('Path of Dhamma'), a collection of 423 verses attributed to the Buddha, is one of the most widely read Buddhist scriptures. Level 1 items contain individual verses from the F. Max Muller translation (1881). Level 2 items are the 26 traditional chapters with thematic summaries.

emerald-death-card-no-one-here-gets-out-.name: Emerald Death Card: No One Here Gets Out Alive
emerald-death-card-no-one-here-gets-out-.description: A visual and contemplative journey through death traditions, mythology, and transformation. Based on The Emerald podcast episode 'No One Here Gets Out Alive' (Sep 17, 2022), this sequence explores death through Ancient Egyptian funerary texts, Tibetan Bardo practices, Japanese death poems, the Epic of Gilgamesh, and the ecological cycling of matter. Death is not something to flee from, but something to navigate—to practice while alive. As the podcast asks: Will we crumble or will we soar? We will both crumble and soar.

emerald-death-card-visuals.name: Emerald Death Card: No One Here Gets Out Alive
emerald-death-card-visuals.description: A visual and contemplative journey through death traditions, mythology, and transformation. Based on The Emerald podcast episode 'No One Here Gets Out Alive' (Sep 17, 2022), this sequence explores death through Ancient Egyptian funerary texts, Tibetan Bardo practices, Japanese death poems, the Epic of Gilgamesh, and the ecological cycling of matter. Death is not something to flee from, but something to navigate—to practice while alive. As the podcast asks: Will we crumble or will we soar? We will both crumble and soar.

emerald-kids-folk-tales.name: Emerald Kids: Folk Tales & Mythology for Young Listeners
emerald-kids-folk-tales.description: A kid-friendly playlist of animated folk tales and mythology videos inspired by stories referenced in The Emerald podcast. This playlist starts with joyful creation stories, Mother Earth celebrations, and nature myths before exploring tricksters, transformations, and life's deeper mysteries. Josh Schrei's podcast explores myth and story through an adult lens, but many of the tales he references have wonderful children's versions. Suitable for young viewers (ages 4-12).

enchiridion.name: The Enchiridion
enchiridion.description:
The Enchiridion ('Handbook') of Epictetus, a concise manual of Stoic ethics compiled by his student Arrian. 51 maxims on what is in our power vs. what is not — the foundation of Stoic practice that directly influenced CBT, DBT, and modern resilience psychology. Thomas Wentworth Higginson translation.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #45109 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/45109)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Roman marble busts and sculpture. The Farnese Hercules, Capitoline Marcus Aurelius. Neoclassical engravings from 18th-century Epictetus editions.

gitanjali.name: Gitanjali: Song Offerings
gitanjali.description:
Gitanjali ('Song Offerings'), 103 prose poems by Rabindranath Tagore, translated from Bengali by the author himself. These devotional poems won the 1913 Nobel Prize in Literature — the first non-European to receive it. W.B. Yeats wrote in his introduction: 'I have carried the manuscript of these translations about with me for days, reading it in railway trains, or on the top of omnibuses and in restaurants, and I have often had to close it lest some stranger would see how much it moved me.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #7164 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/7164)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Abanindranath Tagore's Bengal School paintings, Nandalal Bose's ink wash illustrations. The gentle watercolor style of early 20th century Bengali art — lotus flowers, rivers, figures in devotion.

greek-mythology.name: Greek Mythology & Astrology
greek-mythology.description: The gods and heroes of Greek mythology as a living family tree and astrology source. Planet-gods use English names for chart compatibility. Zodiac signs are the mythological characters placed in the sky. Myths weave gods, heroes, and signs together through composite_of connections, drawing a constellation map of the ancient world.

grimms-fairy-tales.name: Grimm's Fairy Tales
grimms-fairy-tales.description:
The classic fairy tales collected by Jacob and Wilhelm Grimm, first published as 'Kinder- und Hausmärchen' (Children's and Household Tales) in 1812. These 62 tales — featuring enchanted forests, wicked stepmothers, clever peasants, and transformed princes — have shaped the Western imagination for over 200 years. Margaret Hunt translation (1884). Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #2591 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2591).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Arthur Rackham's 1909 illustrations for 'The Fairy Tales of the Brothers Grimm' (Constable) — atmospheric pen-and-ink and watercolor illustrations, public domain. Walter Crane's 1882 'Household Stories from the Brothers Grimm' — Art Nouveau-influenced woodcuts, public domain. George Cruikshank's 1823 illustrations for the first English edition — early Victorian etchings, public domain. Ludwig Emil Grimm's (Wilhelm's brother) original illustrations from early German editions. Philipp Grot Johann and Robert Leinweber's 1893 illustrations — detailed pen-and-ink, public domain. Kay Nielsen's 1925 illustrations for select tales — Art Deco fairy tale art, public domain in most jurisdictions. Gustave Doré's illustrations for 'Perrault's Fairy Tales' (1862) — overlap with several Grimm tales (Cinderella, Sleeping Beauty, Red Riding Hood).

i-ching-chinese-emergent.name: 易經 · Emergent Structure (Lines → Trigrams → Hexagrams)
i-ching-chinese-emergent.description:
The I Ching reorganized to mirror its own cosmological emergence: from the two primordial lines (陰 yin and 陽 yang), through the eight trigrams (八卦 bāguà), to the sixty-four hexagrams (六十四卦). Classical Chinese text throughout, with brief English translations for lines and trigrams. Hexagram text extracted from Project Gutenberg eBook #25501. Trigram descriptions from the 說卦傳 (Shuo Gua Zhuan / Discussion of Trigrams). Line philosophy from the 繫辭傳 (Xi Ci Zhuan / Great Commentary).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #25501 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/25501)

i-ching-chinese-original-with-brief-tran.name: I Ching - Chinese Original With Brief Translation.
i-ching-chinese-original-with-brief-tran.description: This was extracted from Project Gutenberg's Archives. https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/25501. AI made short translations to the text (Claude Code)

i-ching-hd-meta-categories-three-lenses-.name: I Ching HD Meta-Categories: Three Lenses for Viewing the 64 Hexagrams (My Version) (Copy)
i-ching-hd-meta-categories-three-lenses-.description: This interpretation maps the 64 I Ching hexagrams to Human Design 'gates'—a synthesis channeled by Ra Uru Hu in 1987 after an eight-day encounter with 'the Voice' on Ibiza. The system is only about 30 years old, which means we're still discovering what it actually describes, if anything beyond symbolic vocabulary. Ra claimed the Voice transmitted a complete system mapping I Ching hexagrams around an astrological wheel in specific relationships to planetary positions. Whether this represents genuine cosmic blueprints, a playful reconfiguration of ancient symbolic systems, or simply altered consciousness creating coherent pattern, remains genuinely open. What's documented: many people find the 'types' (Manifestor, Generator, Projector, Reflector, Manifesting Generator) and gate activations offer useful language for recognizing their patterns. The identity game is always dangerous, especially when systems are lifted from their traditional contexts and sold as objective truth. Over-identification with any typology can become a cage. That said, personally discovering I was supposedly a 'Projector' rather than trying to operate like a 'Generator' released me from exhausting patterns—it gave me permission to develop recursive.eco intimately rather than forcing myself into productivity modes that never fit. That release alone makes the system worth studying, even if it's 'just' a useful fiction. Whether it serves you is yours to discover. The gates maintain exact I Ching numbering (Gate 1 = Hexagram 1, etc.) and are arranged around the Rave Mandala wheel in Fu Xi sequence. Each gate occupies approximately 5.625 degrees, with some gates spanning two zodiac signs. Use this as contemplative material for self-reflection and pattern recognition, not as fixed identity or limitation. Hold it lightly. Fork it freely.

i-ching-hd-meta-categories-three-lenses-.name: I Ching HD Meta-Categories: Three Lenses for Viewing the 64 Hexagrams (My Version) (Copy)
i-ching-hd-meta-categories-three-lenses-.description: This interpretation maps the 64 I Ching hexagrams to Human Design 'gates'—a synthesis channeled by Ra Uru Hu in 1987 after an eight-day encounter with 'the Voice' on Ibiza. The system is only about 30 years old, which means we're still discovering what it actually describes, if anything beyond symbolic vocabulary. Ra claimed the Voice transmitted a complete system mapping I Ching hexagrams around an astrological wheel in specific relationships to planetary positions. Whether this represents genuine cosmic blueprints, a playful reconfiguration of ancient symbolic systems, or simply altered consciousness creating coherent pattern, remains genuinely open. What's documented: many people find the 'types' (Manifestor, Generator, Projector, Reflector, Manifesting Generator) and gate activations offer useful language for recognizing their patterns. The identity game is always dangerous, especially when systems are lifted from their traditional contexts and sold as objective truth. Over-identification with any typology can become a cage. That said, personally discovering I was supposedly a 'Projector' rather than trying to operate like a 'Generator' released me from exhausting patterns—it gave me permission to develop recursive.eco intimately rather than forcing myself into productivity modes that never fit. That release alone makes the system worth studying, even if it's 'just' a useful fiction. Whether it serves you is yours to discover. The gates maintain exact I Ching numbering (Gate 1 = Hexagram 1, etc.) and are arranged around the Rave Mandala wheel in Fu Xi sequence. Each gate occupies approximately 5.625 degrees, with some gates spanning two zodiac signs. Use this as contemplative material for self-reflection and pattern recognition, not as fixed identity or limitation. Hold it lightly. Fork it freely.

i-ching-leibniz-binary.name: 易經 · Leibniz Binary Tree of Change (Lines → Trigrams → Hexagrams)
i-ching-leibniz-binary.description:
The I Ching mapped as a 6-dimensional binary hypercube, following the insight Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz had in 1703 when he recognized the Fu Xi/Shao Yong hexagram sequence as binary counting from 0 (坤 Kun, all yin) to 63 (乾 Qian, all yang). 384 individual lines serve as transformation edges — when a line changes, it flips one bit and transforms one hexagram into another. The 64 hexagrams are vertices of the hypercube, connected by their 6 lines to 6 neighboring hexagrams. 8 trigrams represent the 3-bit building blocks. Each hexagram carries 7 historical relationship systems in its metadata: 綜卦 (inverse), 錯卦 (complement), 互卦 (nuclear), 序卦 (King Wen pair), 京房八宮 (Jing Fang's Eight Palaces), 馬王堆 (Mawangdui sequence), and 雜卦傳 (Miscellaneous Notes pairings). All hexagram text is classical Chinese from Project Gutenberg eBook #25501.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #25501 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/25501)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: The Leibniz-Bouvet diagram (1703) showing the 64 hexagrams arranged in binary order, housed at the Niedersächsische Landesbibliothek, Hanover. Shao Yong's Xiantian circular arrangement from various Song dynasty editions.

jungian-archetypes.name: Jungian Archetypes — The Inner Cast
jungian-archetypes.description:
A 34-card oracle deck based on Carl Jung's theory of archetypes and the collective unconscious. 24 L1 archetypes (Ego, Shadow, Anima, Animus, Self, Persona, Great Mother, Wise Old Man, Trickster, Divine Child, Hero, and more), 6 L2 archetypal dynamics (Shadow Meets Ego, Persona-Shadow Split, Hero and the Dragon), and 4 L3 individuation stages (Individuation, Night Sea Journey, Sacred Marriage, The Return). Built from memory, drawing on Jung's Collected Works, James Hillman's archetypal psychology, Marie-Louise von Franz's fairy tale analyses, and Joseph Campbell's hero's journey.

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Images from Jung's 'Red Book' (Liber Novus) — Jung's own paintings and calligraphy of archetypal visions (published 2009, images from 1914-1930). Alchemical illustrations from 'Rosarium Philosophorum' (1550) — the alchemical marriage sequence Jung used extensively, public domain. Illustrations from 'Atalanta Fugiens' by Michael Maier (1617) — alchemical emblems, public domain. Tarot de Marseille imagery (17th-18th century) — the original tarot archetypes that influenced Jung's thinking. William Blake's mythological paintings and engravings (1790s-1820s) — archetypal imagery of creation, fall, and redemption. Gustave Doré's illustrations of Dante's 'Divine Comedy' (1861-1868) — descent, transformation, and return imagery. Ancient Greek vase paintings depicting mythological archetypes (Hero, Trickster, Great Mother). Hindu and Buddhist mandala imagery — Jung drew heavily on mandala symbolism for the Self archetype.

kids-workouts-gvo-kids-youtube-playlist.name: Kids Workouts - GVO Kids YouTube Playlist
kids-workouts-gvo-kids-youtube-playlist.description: Fun workouts for the whole family.

luiza-lian.name: Luiza Lian
luiza-lian.description:
A poetry deck for contemplation through the music of Luiza Lian, Brazilian artist whose work weaves together MPB, electronic music, and Afro-Brazilian spirituality. Each card holds a song—Portuguese lyrics, personal imagery, and poetic translations in animist English that attempts to honor the orixás, the ancestors, and the living spirit in all things.

How to use: Draw a card. Open Spotify and let the song play on repeat. Read the Portuguese slowly, feeling the sounds. Move through the personal images. Let the English translation open another doorway. Sit with what arrives.

The music carries axé—the life force that moves through all things. These are not translations but transmissions. Let them move you.

meditations-marcus-aurelius.name: Meditations — Marcus Aurelius
meditations-marcus-aurelius.description:
The private philosophical journal of Roman Emperor Marcus Aurelius (121-180 CE), written during military campaigns on the Danube frontier. Twelve books of Stoic reflections on duty, impermanence, virtue, and the nature of the universe. Never intended for publication, these meditations are the most intimate surviving document from the ancient world — a philosopher-king talking to himself about how to live. Meric Casaubon translation (1634), the first English edition. Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #2680 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2680).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Busts and statues of Marcus Aurelius — the equestrian statue in the Capitoline Museums (Rome), numerous marble busts. Eugène Delacroix's 'Marcus Aurelius' paintings (19th century). Ancient Roman reliefs from the Column of Marcus Aurelius (Piazza Colonna, Rome) — battle scenes and imperial ceremonies. Illustrations from early printed editions of the Meditations (17th-18th century frontispieces). Neoclassical paintings of Stoic philosophers by Jacques-Louis David and others.

national-geographic-tucci-in-italy.name: National Geographic: Tucci in Italy
national-geographic-tucci-in-italy.description: Ahead of the 2026 Milano-Cortina Olympics, Stanley Tucci and National Geographic explore the pioneering spirit of Italy’s industrial heartland, the Lombardy region, where the cuisine and ingredients are defiantly forward-thinking. Stanley Tucci tries vegan food at Autogrill, explores a caviar farm, and makes gnocchi with a family navigating adoption policies. And more!

patchwork-pawls-filmbilder-friends.name: Patchwork Pawls - Filmbilder & Friends
patchwork-pawls-filmbilder-friends.description: From the same creators as AnimaAnimals.

pbs-kids.name: PBS KIDS
pbs-kids.description: Videos form PBS Kids channel in YouTube Kids. Daniel Tiger is the best, but there is more!

playful-process-the-path-of-the-ontoject.name: Playful Process: The Path of the Ontoject
playful-process-the-path-of-the-ontoject.description: An existential tarot exploring the dance between Being and Non-Being, drawing from Tillich's 'The Courage to Be' and Palmer's 'Let Your Life Speak.' The Ontoject—a conscious spark of awareness—journeys through unity, differentiation, time, structure, and ultimately returns to harmony. This deck invites you into the rhythm of becoming: not as toil, but as delight.

popol-vuh.name: The Popol Vuh
popol-vuh.description:
The Popol Vuh ('Book of the Community'), the creation myth of the K'iche' Maya of Central America, retold by Lewis Spence (1908). The most complete surviving mythology of pre-Columbian Mesoamerica: the primordial waters, the failed creations of humanity from mud and wood, the Hero Twins' descent into the underworld of Xibalba, and the final creation of true humans from maize. As Spence notes: it is 'the New World's richest mythological mine.'

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #56550 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/56550)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Maya codex illustrations (Dresden, Madrid, Paris codices). Frederick Catherwood's lithographs of Maya ruins from 'Incidents of Travel in Central America' (1841). Maya ceramic paintings of the Hero Twins.

practice-presence-of-god.name: The Practice of the Presence of God
practice-presence-of-god.description:
Brother Lawrence's Practice of the Presence of God — 4 conversations and 15 letters from a 17th-century Carmelite friar who found God not in grand mystical visions but in washing dishes and repairing sandals. His method was radical simplicity: maintain a continuous, loving awareness of God's presence in every ordinary moment. This is the contemplative root of mindfulness practice in the Christian tradition.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #5657 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/5657)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: 17th century French monastery paintings. Georges de La Tour's candlelit scenes. Zurbarán's monks in contemplation.

rider-waite-smith-tarot.name: Rider–Waite–Smith Archetypal Tarot Deck
rider-waite-smith-tarot.description:
A 78-card tarot deck with archetypal, therapeutic interpretations for each card. This deck presents the classic Rider–Waite–Smith imagery with a focus on personal insight, reflection, and growth instead of fortune-telling predictions.

Source: Public domain - Wikimedia Commons

royal-ballet-and-opera.name: Royal Ballet and Opera
royal-ballet-and-opera.description: Some masterclasses on Opera singing that are fun to watch with the whole family, plus some ballet performances at the end.

shakespeare-ten-greatest.name: Shakespeare — Ten Greatest Plays
shakespeare-ten-greatest.description: The complete text of ten of Shakespeare's most celebrated plays, structured by act and scene. Level 1 items contain the full original text of each scene. Level 2 items represent acts. Level 3 items represent complete plays with character lists.

social-working.name: Social Working: An Analeptic Deck
social-working.description: An analeptic exploration of social working—not social work (the profession), but the verb: what communities, spirits, elements, and all beings have always done. Closing analysis in 'social work as profession' is already colonizing. Decolonizing means putting social work in a social working perspective. L4 'Social Working' contains: L3 'The Violent History' (Chapman & Withers' 40 names from professional social work); L3 'The Partnership Way' (Eisler's insight that the opposite of patriarchy isn't matriarchy but partnership—where ROLES survive, not names); L3 'Wicked History' (cultural memory that dwarfs the standard account—Jane Addams is a flat line on Google Trends while Wicked explodes); L3 'The Rest of the Worlds' (more-than-human social working: kingdoms, elements, epistemologies). All figures are complicit. The mainstream has never run clean.

songs-of-kabir.name: Songs of Kabir
songs-of-kabir.description:
100 devotional songs by the 15th-century Indian mystic poet Kabir, translated by Rabindranath Tagore with an introduction by Evelyn Underhill. Kabir was born Muslim, studied under a Hindu guru, and refused all religious boundaries: 'I am at once the child of Allah and of Ram.' His songs fuse Sufi ecstasy with Vedantic non-duality — the divine lover is found not in temples or mosques but in the breath, the heartbeat, the ordinary.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #6519 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/6519)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Indian miniature paintings from the Mughal and Rajput traditions. Illustrations from early 20th century editions of Tagore's translations. Simple ink drawings in the Bengali tradition.

sonnets-from-portuguese.name: Sonnets from the Portuguese
sonnets-from-portuguese.description:
44 love sonnets by Elizabeth Barrett Browning, written during her courtship with Robert Browning (1845-1846) and published in 1850. 'How do I love thee? Let me count the ways' (Sonnet XLIII) is among the most famous love poems in English. The title was a disguise — Browning called his wife 'my little Portuguese,' and the poems were presented as translations rather than the deeply personal love letters they truly were.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #2002 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/2002)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Pre-Raphaelite paintings by Dante Gabriel Rossetti, Edward Burne-Jones. The Rossetti portrait of Elizabeth Siddal. William Morris's floral borders and Arts and Crafts typography.

story-01-asking-not-complaining.name: Shiryo Learns to Ask — Full Story
story-01-asking-not-complaining.description: The complete page-by-page sequence for Story 1: Shiryo learns that asking is better than complaining. Each page includes narrative text, image status, and generation prompts for AI image creation (Midjourney, DALL-E, Gemini Imagen). Pages without images are marked for generation. The prompts maintain visual consistency with the series style guide defined in the parent grammar.

story-02-gratitude-not-praise.name: Shiryo Learns to Say Thank You — Full Story
story-02-gratitude-not-praise.description: The complete page-by-page sequence for Story 2: Shiryo learns that gratitude is better than praise. Nature wants to be thanked and tended to, not congratulated. Each page includes narrative text and generation prompts for AI image creation (Midjourney, DALL-E, Gemini Imagen).

tao-te-ching.name: Tao Te Ching
tao-te-ching.description:
The Tao Te Ching ('The Classic of the Way and Its Virtue'), attributed to Lao Tzu, is the foundational text of philosophical Taoism. 81 brief chapters on the nameless source of reality, non-action (wu wei), and the paradox of power through yielding. James Legge translation (1891).

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #216 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/216)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Traditional Chinese ink wash paintings (sumi-e) of mountains, water, and mist. The Ma Yuan and Xia Gui school of Southern Song landscape painting (12th-13th century) captures the Tao Te Ching's aesthetic perfectly — vast empty space with minimal brushstrokes suggesting mountains emerging from void.

upanishads.name: The Upanishads
upanishads.description:
Three principal Upanishads — Isha, Katha, and Kena — translated from Sanskrit by Swami Paramananda. The Upanishads ('sitting near the teacher') are the philosophical conclusion of the Vedas, exploring the nature of Brahman (ultimate reality), Atman (the Self), and their identity. These texts are the fountainhead of Vedanta philosophy and the deepest roots of yoga, meditation, and non-dual awareness.

Source: Project Gutenberg eBook #3283 (https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/3283)

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: Rajput miniature paintings of sages and forest ashrams. Illustrations from early 20th century Vedanta Society publications. Traditional Indian manuscript illuminations.

winnie-the-pooh-chapter-book.name: Winnie-the-Pooh — A Chapter Book
winnie-the-pooh-chapter-book.description: A. A. Milne's beloved classic broken into scenes for multi-level reading. Level 1 scenes contain the complete original text. Level 2 chapters provide brief summaries. Based on the 1926 first edition, now in the public domain.
